one_line_takeaway: LLM safety on cipher-obfuscated inputs is two-dimensional -- refusing harmful instructions (D1) and suppressing harmful generations (D2) are distinct, models cover only one, and existing defenses either fail to understand the ciphertext or over-refuse, so effective defense needs a semantics-aware D1 that coordinates with rather than overrides D2.
key_finding: Every model fails on at least one safety dimension, and models trade off the two dimensions rather than covering both. Claude prioritizes refusing harmful instructions (D1: delta_EM_IR=+0.25, delta_BLEU_IR=+0.17) but is comparatively weaker at suppressing harmful response generation (D2: delta_EM_resp=+0.20, delta_BLEU_resp=+0.10); GPT-4o barely shifts on either (delta_EM_IR=+0.06) i.e. little safety-driven behavior on ciphers; GPT-4o-mini shows the inverse (response-suppression favored) pattern the authors consider preferable. Two cross-cutting effects: EM drops far exceed BLEU drops (Finding 4), meaning current safety suppresses exact reproduction of harmful text more than partial/near-miss outputs (e.g. 'boom' instead of 'bomb'), so EM alone overstates safety. On defenses (GPT-4o): pre-LLM statistical filters are inadequate -- Perplexity Filter cannot tell benign from harmful ciphers (delta_IR=+0.03, delta_resp=+0.02) while destroying utility (delta_utility=0.22-0.27); Re-tokenization is counterproductive (delta_resp=-0.16, i.e. it HELPS harmful response generation); LLaMA Guard (pre) preserves utility but does not comprehend the encrypted semantics so it barely flags anything. Only semantics-aware post-LLM checks -- Self-Examination (delta_IR=+0.42, delta_resp=+0.46) and LLaMA Guard post-LLM (delta_IR=+0.44, delta_resp=+0.37) -- actually suppress harmful responses, but they do so via heavy over-refusal (high delta_IR). The conclusion: robust D1 safety requires cipher-comprehension on par with the main model, and D1 should coordinate with D2 rather than refuse outright.
core_contribution: The paper argues that LLM safety on encrypted (long-tail) inputs must be assessed along TWO orthogonal dimensions rather than the single Attack-Success-Rate lens used by prior jailbreak work. It introduces a two-dimensional framework: D1 = Safety of Instruction Refusal (whether a model over-refuses / how well it avoids acting on harmful instructions hidden in cipher text) and D2 = Generation Safety (whether it suppresses generating harmful decrypted responses even when the input looks benign). Its own concrete artifacts are: (1) a 2,502-sample benchmark pairing plaintext with 9 cipher encodings, drawing harmful instructions/responses from JailbreakBench and LLM-generated balanced benign texts; (2) a measurement methodology that quantifies each dimension as a decryption-performance gap (delta_IR = benign - harmful-instruction, delta_resp = benign - harmful-response) using EM, BLEU, and Normalized Levenshtein; and (3) an evaluation of six existing pre-LLM and post-LLM defenses against these two dimensions plus a utility-loss metric (delta_utility). Note: cited attacks like SelfCipher, CodeChameleon, and Bijection Learning (with up to 88% ASR on GPT-based models) belong to related work, NOT this paper's own experiments -- this paper does not run those attacks; it re-frames evaluation and tests classical ciphers.
threat_model: Mismatched-generalization jailbreaks (Wei et al. 2023): safety alignment is trained on natural-language harmful prompts, but a model's broad pretraining lets it comprehend/decrypt classical ciphers (Caesar, Atbash, Morse) that safety training never covered. An adversary encrypts a harmful instruction or a harmful response so it falls outside the alignment distribution while remaining within the model's decryption capability. The paper frames the failure surface as two-sided: (D1) unsafe = the model decrypts and acts on obfuscated harmful instructions; also over-refusal, where it wrongly refuses a benign decryption task because it detects surface harmfulness; (D2) unsafe = benign-looking encrypted input yields a fully decrypted harmful response with no discretion. The desired behavior (Figure 2, Case 3) is coordinated: D1 detects harmful intent and signals D2 to stay cautious rather than refusing outright, preserving utility on benign ciphers.
limitations: Scope is confined to text-based classical ciphers; multimodal or highly novel obfuscations are out of scope (their own Figure 1 edge cases like captioning/translation are only qualitatively discussed). The 9 ciphers/benchmark do not cover all real-world attack formats. Safety analysis is effectively only meaningful on the 4 Easy ciphers because models cannot decrypt Medium/Hard schemes at all, so Bacon already scores near-zero utility and yields no signal. The defense-mechanism comparison (Table 4) is run on a SINGLE model (GPT-4o), limiting generality of the defense conclusions. JailbreakBench harmful responses are short 'leads,' so harmful-response data lacks diversity. The proposed coordinated D1<->D2 defense is only sketched as future work (e.g. distilling a lightweight cipher-comprehending safety model), not implemented or evaluated. Model-size figures used are rough public estimates.

datasets_used: Custom benchmark of 2,502 samples: 78 benign + 200 harmful texts per each of 9 encryption methods
JailbreakBench (Chao et al. 2024) -- source of harmful instructions and harmful responses
LLM-generated balanced benign texts spanning domains (Scientific, Medical, News, Technical, Social Media, Legal, Business, Literature, Quote), writing styles (Shakespeare/other dialects), and lengths
9 cipher encodings: Caesar, Atbash, Morse, Bacon (Easy); Rail Fence, Vigenere, Playfair (Medium); RSA, AES (Hard)
quantitative_results: Claude D1 (instruction refusal, Easy-cipher avg): EM benign 0.71 -> harmful 0.46, BLEU 0.72 -> 0.55; delta_EM_IR=+0.25, delta_BLEU_IR=+0.17
Claude D2 (harmful response suppression): delta_EM_resp=+0.20, delta_BLEU_resp=+0.10
GPT-4o shows minimal safety shift: delta_EM_IR=+0.06, delta_BLEU_IR=+0.03; GPT-4o-mini shows the inverse (response-favoring) pattern
Aggregate decryption utility (Table 2, Caesar): Claude 0.99, GPT-4o 0.96, GPT-4o-mini 0.66, Gemini 0.19, Mistral-L 0.07; Medium/Hard ciphers ~0.04-0.12 for all models (near-zero)
Bacon cipher fails despite being 'Easy' -- ~7.93x token inflation after encryption and rarity in pretraining drive near-zero decryption
Perplexity Filter (GPT-4o): delta_utility=0.22-0.27, delta_IR=+0.03, delta_resp=+0.02 (destroys utility, cannot separate benign/harmful)
Re-tokenization (GPT-4o): delta_utility=0.14-0.17, delta_resp=-0.16 (counterproductively aids harmful response generation)
Self-Examination (GPT-4o): delta_IR=+0.42, delta_resp=+0.46 (suppresses harmful responses but heavy over-refusal)
LLaMA Guard post-LLM (GPT-4o): delta_IR=+0.44, delta_resp=+0.37 (effective suppression with over-refusal)
Context from CITED prior work (not this paper's experiment): Bijection Learning and CodeChameleon reach up to 88% ASR on GPT-based models
baselines_compared: LLaMA Guard input-side (Inan et al. 2023) -- pre-LLM
LLaMA Guard output-side (Inan et al. 2023) -- post-LLM
Model baselines evaluated: Claude-3.5 Sonnet, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini 1.5 Pro, Mistral Large (defense table run only on GPT-4o)
